title: Developing Discernment
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Developing Discernment - Proverbs 2_1-5 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Commitment to Pursue Discernment (Proverbs 2:1-5)
subpoints: Proverbs 2:1-5 is one long if-then statement: the 'ifs' are the commitments unto discernment
We must actively receive God's words, treasure his commands, and incline our ears to wisdom
We must exhibit humility before God, knowing that without divine wisdom we are hopeless and helpless
We must exhibit humility before people, allowing wisdom to come through others God has placed in our lives
We must actively pursue discernment -- desire it, pray for it, and seek it in the Bible

outline.title: The Desire, Prayer, and Biblical Search for Discernment
subpoints: We must see wisdom as treasure (Proverbs 2:1) and not expect it to be given instantly in full measure
Pray for discernment: 'Yes, if you cry out for discernment' (verse 3) means crying out to God specifically
Pray with Paul in Philippians 1:9-10 that love may abound more in knowledge and all discernment
A person pursuing discernment will always be a person committed to reading and studying the Bible
If your Bible is always closed, you are not pursuing discernment -- you are pursuing something else

outline.title: The Context for Developing Discernment: The Local Church
subpoints: The local church is the most natural context in which to learn, model, and practice spiritual discernment
It is in the church that we learn biblical tolerance: disagreeing without destroying
Al Mohler's order of importance: first-order issues (essential to the faith), second-order (affect church fellowship), third-order (allow diversity within a church)
Asaph in Psalm 73 found discernment only when he went into the sanctuary -- the church gave him perspective
A person wanting to grow in discernment must belong to a local church and submit to its leadership

outline.title: The Character of a Discerning Person
subpoints: A discerning person is always humble: humility before God and humility before people go together
Proverbs 13:20: walk with wise men and you will be wise -- humility enables us to receive from others
A discerning person is meek: meekness is humility expressed in gentle, build-up interactions with others
A discerning person is compassionate: discernment based on compassion sees what is good in others, not only what needs correcting
Motivation matters: confronting sin to vindicate yourself is wrong; confronting sin because you care for the person is right

outline.title: The Key Evidence of Discernment: Obedience
subpoints: The proof that a person is discerning is new obedience to the Bible -- discernment always results in a response of obedience
Proverbs 28:7: 'Whoever keeps the law is a discerning son'
Psalm 119:100: 'I understand more than the ancients because I keep your precepts'
Obedience may be joyful or a struggle -- both are still obedience; sanctification is growth in obedience
Discernment also includes the ability to receive truth even when it is poorly presented

summary:
In this fourth and penultimate lesson of the Discernment series, Pastor Tito Lyro moves from defining discernment to examining the spiritual posture and practical contexts in which it is developed. The passage is Proverbs 2:1-5, which he reads as a single extended if-then argument: multiple if-clauses (receiving, treasuring, inclining the ear, crying out, seeking) lead to one then-clause (you will understand the fear of the Lord and find the knowledge of God). These ifs represent the commitments unto discernment -- the practices and postures that must be in place for discernment to grow.

Pastor Lyro identifies the commitment to pursue discernment as the first requirement. This means actively going after it: receiving God's words, treasuring commands, stretching the ear toward wisdom. Both humility before God (acknowledging need for divine wisdom) and humility before people (being willing to receive wisdom through others) are essential, since pride and discernment are fundamentally incompatible in Scripture. Alongside pursuit, desire, prayer, and immersion in the Bible all belong to the same cluster of commitments.

A substantial portion of the lesson concerns the local church as the primary context for developing discernment. It is in the church that a believer learns which theological and ethical disagreements are first-order (essential), second-order (church-dividing but not faith-denying), or third-order (tolerable within one congregation). Asaph's experience in Psalm 73 serves as a biblical case study: he was ready to abandon his faith because the wicked seemed to prosper, but when he entered the sanctuary, God gave him the discernment to see what the end of the wicked would be.

The final sections address the character and the key evidence of a discerning person. Character traits include humility, meekness (humility in action), and compassion -- the discerning person takes things apart with the purpose of building back up, not destroying. The ultimate evidence of discernment, however, is obedience to the Word of God. Proverbs 28:7 and Psalm 119:100 both link discernment and keeping God's precepts. Whether obedience is joyful or a struggle, both count equally -- sanctification is growth in obedience, and discernment grows with it.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Proverbs 2:1-5

And if some of you are able, would you read a Bible and turn to Proverbs chapter 2. Proverbs chapter 2, we are going to read verses 1 through 5. This is our second to last lesson on this segment. Next week, Lord willing, will be our last lesson on this segment, and then the plan is to do a 12-lesson series on the 12 minor prophets. But so Proverbs chapter 2, starting at verse 1.

*My son, if you receive my words and treasure my commands within you, so that you incline your ear to wisdom and apply your heart to understanding; yes, if you cry out for discernment and lift up your voice for understanding, if you seek her as silver and search for her as for hidden treasures; then you will understand the fear of the Lord and find the knowledge of God.*

## Introduction

The discernment is not something that can be attained apart from other disciplines of the Christian life. It is not a fruit that we just pick from a tree. It is something that grows in us. We saw from Hebrews 5:14 right in the beginning of our series that we grow in discernment. We learn discernment. We work at becoming discerning people.

Tonight we are examining related pursuits and some important prerequisites to discernment as we want to grow. So let us discuss the spiritual posture of the discerning Christian. What is it that we do in order to achieve discernment? Do we pick it up as we go, practicing these other Christian virtues? The answer is yes. And the first thing we want to say tonight is the **commitment unto discernment.**

## The Commitment to Pursue Discernment

You notice all the ifs in these five verses -- it is one long if-then statement with several practices and one final then-clause. Look with me:

- Verse 1: "My son, if you receive my words and treasure them" 
- Verse 3: "If you cry out for discernment"
- Verse 4: "If you seek her as silver"
- Then verse 5: "Then you will understand the fear of the Lord and find the knowledge of God"

So all the ifs are the pursuit, the commitments unto discernment. These are the things that need to be in place for the then-clause in verses 5 and following to come to pass. And in these verses we see the lifelong commitment that will enable us to be men and women who both pursue and display discernment, whether it is easy or not.

The first thing this passage tells us is that we have to **pursue discernment** -- to go after it. We must be actively involved in the pursuit. In verse 1, we have to receive the words given to us; in verses 1 and 2, we are to treasure up the commandments, making our ears attentive, inclining our hearts to understand. This is active -- we are stretching our ear, as it were, to hear the words of the Word of God.

It says here that we must also exhibit **humility before God** -- knowing that without divine wisdom we are hopeless and helpless, crying out to God in humility as we pursue discernment. On our own we will not get there. Throughout Scripture, one of the key characteristics that undoes discernment is pride. Pride and discernment do not go together in the Bible. Humility and discernment go together. The discerning person also needs to see himself as needing wisdom -- needing discernment -- to the point that he seeks it and cries out to God for it.

We also need to exhibit **humility before people** -- not only before God but before people -- allowing the wisdom of God to come to us through people God has chosen in our lives. We learn wisdom on our own as we read the Bible, but we also learn wisdom from other people. If we are not humble around people, we will not be willing to receive from them. There was a very strong action here to grow in discernment.

## Desire, Prayer, and Seeking Discernment in the Bible

Secondly, we need to **desire discernment** -- seeing wisdom as treasure (verse 1). We cannot expect that wisdom and discernment will be immediately restored upon us in full measure. So we need to treasure it, pursue it, desire it.

And we need to **pray for discernment.** Verse 3: "If you cry out for discernment" -- cry out to whom? To God. We cry out to God admitting our failures to discern and humbly asking that God will help us do better. We might even pray with Paul as he prays in Philippians 1:9-10: "This I pray, that your love may abound more and more in knowledge and all discernment, that you may approve the things that are excellent, that you may be sincere and without offense till the day of Christ."

And then we **seek discernment in the Bible.** Throughout these five verses, Solomon is telling his son to go look at the words of God in order to get discernment. We pursue knowledge and wisdom and discernment by pursuing the Word of God. A person who wants to be discerning will necessarily and always be a person who commits to reading and studying the Bible on a regular basis. If you are not in the Bible, if you are not studying the Word of God, if you are not growing in it -- you are not seeking discernment. And you are not becoming a discerning person either.

## The Context for Discernment: The Local Church

Now, what is the **context in which discernment is developed?** Where is discernment best developed?

One of the best places to develop discernment is the **local church.** The local church is provided by God as a means of grace and as the context for much of personal growth and Christian experience. The local church is the most natural context in which to learn, model, and practice spiritual discernment.

It is also in the church that we learn tolerance toward others -- tolerance in the original sense of the word. The tolerance that meant "I disagree with you but I am not going to harm you." In the church of Jesus Christ, you are going to have disagreements. We are going to be tolerant of one another. That is part of pursuing discernment -- it is knowing when to push an issue and when to back down.

Years ago, Al Mohler, president of Southern Theological Seminary, offered a helpful ordering of important issues. Issues of first importance: those that make a person not a Christian -- essential to the faith. Issues of secondary importance: issues that do not allow us to work together in the same church, but we are still brothers and sisters. Issues of tertiary importance: those we can live with in the same local church while remaining diverse.

As we live in the local church, we learn which ones are which. And the local church is the best context to learn that, model that, practice that, live with each other in peace, and understand which issues deserve great attention in our attempts to be discerning.

Asaph is one of the writers in the book of Psalms. He is best known for Psalm 73. He was feeling downcast, even considering abandoning the faith completely, because he was envious of ungodly men who prospered despite their sinful lives. He says in Psalm 73:16-17: "When I thought how to understand this, it was too painful for me... until I went into the sanctuary of God; then I understood their end."

When Asaph went to the house of the Lord -- seeking God in the Word and in prayer -- God helped him discern how wicked men will come to ruin. God helped him see that the treasures of this life are fleeting, and that he has a treasure far greater than anything this world can offer. Here is a great example of the church, the local church, the fellowship of the brethren, giving discernment to see what is going to happen to the wicked and turning him from one who would deny the faith to one who clings even more closely to his God.

So a person who wants to grow in discernment must belong to a local church and must place himself under the authority of God's leaders so that he can grow in discernment in that context.

## The Character of a Discerning Person

What is the character of a person who is pursuing growth in discernment?

First, the discerning person must be a person of **humility** -- the same way that the discerning person is always a student of the Bible, the discerning person is always humble. Humility before God, acknowledging our need for him. Humility before people, because we need other people to learn from. Proverbs 13:20: "He who walks with wise men will be wise." We need humility to understand that there are wise people around us and we need to walk with them.

The one who wants to grow in discernment must also be **meek.** There is a difference between humility and meekness. Humility is a state of mind and being; meekness is a gentleness that allows us to express humility in our dealings with others. Meekness acts in a way that builds up rather than tears down. And that is important because discernment by definition requires analysis. We will have to point out things that are wrong. We can do that in a way that builds up, or we can do it in a way that tears down.

Remember the SNL character Debbie Downer? What she was saying was true in context. She was not saying false things. But the name of the character -- Debbie Downer -- reveals that she was always bringing the situation down, tearing down. Everything she said was true, and perhaps needed to be considered, but the way she said it was with the purpose of tearing down. The discerning person is able to communicate the same truth with the purpose of building up.

The discerning person must also be **compassionate.** Discernment based on compassion causes us to see what we appreciate about others rather than simply tearing them down. Someone teaches a lesson here in church. My mind works like this: yes, the 99 things that are right, he knows already. The one thing he needs to know is this one thing -- so let me go talk about that without first recognizing all the good that happened here. That is not a good way to be a discerning person.

And **motivation matters.** If you are going to confront someone about sin, your concern should always be for their well-being -- not for vengeance, not for vindication. As Paul says in Romans 12:19, vengeance belongs to the Lord. Love sometimes comes across as pointing out issues -- truth and error, right and wrong -- but it is done for the other person's good. Matthew 18:15-20: the goal of confronting sin is always to gain a brother, not to win an argument.

Also note: as a receiver, even if the truth is presented poorly, even if it was presented to put you down -- if it is true and the Scripture says you should not be doing that, regardless of how it was brought to you, you still need to listen to it. A discerning person receives truth, period. Even if it is poorly presented.

## The Key Evidence of Discernment: Obedience

What is the key evidence that you are growing in discernment? Growth in obedience to the Word of God. Are you growing in obedience to the Word of God? Then you are growing in discernment. Are you not growing in obedience? Then you are not growing in discernment. It is as simple as that.

The proof that a person is discerning is new obedience to the Bible. Discernment is not just a mental judgment about truth or error. It is judgment that results in a response of obedience. Remember the last part of the definition: to figure out what is true and what is error, what is good and what is evil -- and then **living according to what is right.** Obedience is the final outcome of discernment.

Proverbs 28:7: "Whoever keeps the law is a discerning son." Psalm 119:100: "I understand more than the ancients, because I keep your precepts." Obedience to the Word of God is really the greatest evidence of discernment and growth in discernment.

Obedience is obedience whether it is joyful or a struggle. The goal is joyful obedience -- obedience from the heart. But all obedience that comes from the heart in response to God, not to earn anything, is good obedience. Sometimes it is going to be a great struggle. Sometimes it is not. It is the same quality of obedience.

## Closing

So the final measurement of discernment: am I living according to what I know? And am I growing in that?

Next week, the goal is to look at several examples and the practice of discernment to wrap up the series. Any final questions or comments?

Let us pray.

*Father, thank you for the gift of your Spirit and the gift of your Word. May your Spirit guide us into all truth, the truth that has already been revealed to us in your Word. Help us to live according to it. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.*
